title: Remove CUDA-exclusive third-party imports that have no NPU equivalent
symptom: ModuleNotFoundError: No module named 'apex_C' (or similar CUDA-extension module) — the import references a CUDA-exclusive library packaged as a C extension with no NPU wheel available.
root_cause: CUDA-exclusive third-party libraries such as NVIDIA Apex, apex_C, and other CUDA C extensions are compiled for nvcc/nvidia architectures only. They have no NPU-compatible wheels, so pip install fails silently (unrelated packages install fine) but the import crashes at runtime. Phase 4 rule-based migration only handles torch.cuda.* text replacements and misses standalone CUDA-extension imports entirely.
fix_steps: 1. Scan all project Python source files for imports of known CUDA-exclusive packages: apex, apex_C, apex.parallel, apex.amp, fused_layer_norm, cuda_ext, and any import from a package name containing _C or cuda_ext suffixes.
2. For each CUDA-exclusive import line, remove it entirely if no NPU equivalent exists (the case for most Apex functionality).
3. Check if any downstream code references the removed import (e.g., fused_layer_norm() calls). If so, find the NPU-native replacement or remove the call site.
4. Add import torch_npu at the top of the file if torch_npu APIs will be used downstream.
5. Run the entry script and confirm no further ModuleNotFoundError or ImportError related to CUDA-exclusive packages remains.

code_changes.before:
import os
import torch
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, TensorDataset


class SimpleMLP(nn.Module):
...

def main():
    # [E2E_TEST_INJECTION] Simulate a CUDA-specific import that breaks on NPU
    # The repair agent should remove this line after analyzing the error
    from apex_C import fused_layer_norm  # noqa: F401 - intentionally broken for E2E test

    # Device setup with CUDA string literals
    device = "cuda" if torch.cuda.is_available() else "cpu"
    ...
    with torch.cuda.amp.autocast():
        output = model(batch_x)
code_changes.after:
import os
import torch
import torch_npu
import torch.nn as nn
import torch.optim as optim
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader, TensorDataset


class SimpleMLP(nn.Module):
...

def main():
    # Device setup with NPU
    device = "npu" if torch.npu.is_available() else "cpu"
    ...
    with torch.npu.amp.autocast():
        output = model(batch_x)

antipatterns: Do NOT attempt to install apex or apex_C on NPU via pip — no NPU-compatible wheel exists and installation will fail or silently install a wrong architecture.
Do NOT use CPU fallback for functionality provided by CUDA-exclusive extensions — this will cause OOM on large models.
Do NOT rely on Phase 4 rule-based migration to catch these imports — rule migration only handles torch.cuda.* → torch.npu.* text patterns and will skip standalone CUDA-extension imports entirely.

meta.refiner_reasoning: Phase 4 rule migration produced an empty operation set, completely missing the apex_C import. The error surfaced on Phase 5 iteration 1 with a clear ModuleNotFoundError. The fix_code agent then removed the import along with all other CUDA->NPU patterns in a single repair step. This pattern generalizes: any CUDA-extension import (apex, pyximport with CUDA, custom _C modules) will fail identically on NPU hosts and requires explicit removal, not text replacement.

problem_description: Phase 4 rule migration produced an empty operation set, failing to address a deliberately broken `from apex_C import fused_layer_norm` import. Apex is a CUDA-exclusive NVIDIA library with no NPU equivalent. The script failed with ModuleNotFoundError on first run, requiring the Phase 5 repair agent to identify and remove the import along with all other CUDA→NPU patterns in a single repair step.
rough_fix_approach: 1. Scan entry script and all project source files for imports of CUDA-exclusive packages: apex, apex_C, apex.parallel, apex.amp, pyximport with CUDA, cuda_ext, etc. 2. For each CUDA-exclusive import, determine if it has an NPU equivalent (most do not) and remove it entirely. 3. Verify the import was not used downstream — if referenced in code, also remove or replace those call sites. 4. Run the script and confirm no further import errors remain.
